title: Lifecycle-aware validation enforcement and steering quality
description:
Make validation enforcement fire at the right lifecycle moments and make findings actionable enough that an agent can self-correct, so structural validation actually steers agents instead of training them to ignore it.

## Background

A June 2026 evaluation of the SDD example ruleset in an isolated repo found that the graph rules (criteria coverage, req/satisfies integrity) report error-severity findings during the entire legitimate planning phase, while the one moment they should bite is unenforced: an epic with declared `req:` labels, an uncovered `[hard]` criterion, and zero children transitions to `done` with exit 0. A fabricated `req:` id and a not-yet-implemented one produce identical findings. Schema findings for unparsed sections are content-free (`[] has less than 1 item`). The engine also lacks primitives for requirement-to-check mapping, evidence freshness, and cross-graph analysis, and there is no way to measure whether rule changes improve agent steering.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] A correctly authored in-flight parent issue with declared `req:` labels and incomplete children produces zero error-severity findings from `jit validate`
- [ ] An issue cannot transition to `done` while an enforcing graph rule fails for it (blocked, exit 4, findings shown)
- [ ] A `req:` label whose id is absent from the canonical criteria section yields a finding distinct from a declared-but-unsatisfied one
- [ ] Schema findings name the failing section/field; the empty-section case states that items must be Markdown bullets
- [ ] Criteria-to-check mapping, gate-result recency, and repo-wide graph scope are each available as engine primitives and demonstrated by an example ruleset under docs/examples/
- [ ] A non-software example ruleset demonstrates the engine on a hierarchy without epics or milestones
- [ ] A deterministic steering-scenario suite runs in CI, and an LLM-agent eval measures iterations-to-green against the same scenarios
